behaviors.description: Migrations are applied in ascending numeric order determined by the filename prefix
behaviors.justification: Must apply migrations to a real (test) PostgreSQL database and verify the _migrations table records them in the correct order. Mocking file listing alone wouldn't catch bugs in parsing filenames or actual execution order where migrations silently apply out of sequence.
behaviors.test_description: Create migrations 001.sql, 003.sql, 002.sql (in filesystem order) with distinct side effects (e.g., creating separate tables). Run the runner once. Verify _migrations table shows [001, 002, 003] in that order and database schema reflects correct application order.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test requires real database state inspection to verify order; mocking would only verify call order, not actual execution.

behaviors.description: A migration that has already been applied is not re-executed on a subsequent run
behaviors.justification: Idempotency requires verifying that database state persists between runs and the runner correctly checks the _migrations table before re-executing. Pure mocking wouldn't catch bugs where the idempotency check is missing or incorrectly compares migration names.
behaviors.test_description: Run the runner once against a test database (e.g., apply migrations 001.sql and 002.sql). Run it again. Verify _migrations table still has exactly 2 entries, no duplicate rows, and no side effects (e.g., re-created objects) from re-execution.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test requires persistent database state between two consecutive runs; mocks cannot simulate state persistence accurately.

behaviors.description: When a migration statement fails, its changes are rolled back automatically and no further migrations in the same run are attempted
behaviors.justification: Transaction rollback behavior is database-specific and depends on real PostgreSQL transaction semantics. Mocking rollback would hide failures where transactions aren't properly scoped, committed, or rolled back. This is a high-impact failure that mocks commonly miss.
behaviors.test_description: Create migrations: 001.sql (succeeds, creates table A), 002.sql (fails with SQL error), 003.sql (would succeed, creates table B). Run the runner. Verify: 001 recorded in _migrations and table A exists; 002 not recorded in _migrations and never executed; 003 never attempted; table B does not exist.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test must verify actual database state (table existence, _migrations contents) to confirm rollback occurred; mocks can easily hide transaction bugs.

behaviors.description: When two instances of the runner start at the same time against the same database, each migration is applied exactly once across both instances
behaviors.justification: True race condition detection requires real OS-level concurrency and real database locking. Threading in a single process or mocking both wouldn't reliably expose bugs where two processes both pass the idempotency check simultaneously, or where the lock is missing/broken. Database-level pessimistic or optimistic locking must be verified under real scheduling.
behaviors.test_description: Spawn two runner processes targeting the same test database, with a 50ms stagger to maximize overlap. Verify both processes complete successfully. Inspect _migrations table and verify: each migration appears exactly once, no duplicate entries, no deadlock timeout, and both processes' exit codes indicate success.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test requires real OS processes and real database locks; cannot be reliably simulated with threads or mocks—concurrency bugs hide in real scheduling.

behaviors.description: On the very first run against a fresh database, the _migrations table is created before any migration file is applied
behaviors.justification: Requires a real (test) database without the _migrations table to verify correct table creation and initialization order. Mocking table creation wouldn't catch issues where the DDL is wrong, required columns are missing, or the table is created after migrations begin executing.
behaviors.test_description: Start with a fresh PostgreSQL test database with no _migrations table. Run the runner. Verify: _migrations table exists, has correct schema (id, name, applied_at columns), contains all applied migrations, and no migration was partially applied before the table existed.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test needs a genuinely fresh database state; mocking table operations would hide DDL bugs and bootstrap ordering bugs.
